_meta.purpose: Phase 1 pipeline test — Client #1 reference case

business_information.owner_name: Dr. Lauryn Richard, DNP, APRN, PMHNP-BC
business_information.industry: Mental health care / Outpatient psychiatric services
business_information.business_stage: Startup — launched January 2026 as telehealth practice; planning physical location by 2027.
business_information.funding_purpose: Grant / Property acquisition and build-out of an integrated outpatient mental health clinic with urgent care psychiatric model and therapeutic plant nursery/sanctuary.
business_information.location: 1512 Cleary Avenue, Metairie, Louisiana 70001 (Jefferson Parish, greater New Orleans metro)

management_details.owner_background: Dr. Lauryn Richard holds a Doctor of Nursing Practice (DNP) from LSU Health New Orleans (2023), specializing in Psychiatric Mental Health. She is a licensed RN and APRN in Louisiana, board-certified as a Psychiatric Mental Health Nurse Practitioner (PMHNP-BC) through ANCC, and holds an active DEA registration. She has 7 years of clinical psychiatric experience: 6 years as an inpatient psychiatric RN and charge nurse on a 53-bed pediatric unit at Manning Family Children's Hospital, followed by 1 year as an ED psychiatric consultant at the same institution performing psychiatric evaluations and crisis interventions. She is a member of NAMI, the Louisiana State Nurses Association, and the American Nurses Association. She served as Night Shift Representative and Chair of the Psychiatric Inpatient Unit Council (2021–2023), Education Co-Chair of the Advanced Practice Professionals Council (2024–2025), and was inducted into Sigma Theta Tau International Honor Society (2023). Her DNP project on co-occurring mental illness screening was presented at LCMC Research Day 2023. She was a HRSA Behavioral Health Workforce Education and Training Grant recipient (2020–2023).
management_details.management_team: Currently a solo practice with no additional management team. Dr. Richard manages all clinical, administrative, and operational functions. Plans to hire administrative support staff in month 12 and additional mental health professionals as the practice grows.
management_details.hiring_plans: No hires in Year 1 (months 1–11). Administrative assistant ($2,500–$3,000/month) hired in month 12 at physical location launch. Nursery manager ($2,000–$2,500/month) hired months 12–13. Additional psychiatric providers and therapists planned for Year 2–3 as patient volume grows.

product_service_summary.services_offered: Comprehensive psychiatric evaluations (60–90 minutes, initial visit). Medication management appointments (15–60 minutes, follow-up based on acuity). Crisis support between scheduled appointments. Supportive therapy using Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT), with training underway in trauma-focused approaches and DBT. Specialty focus: ADHD, anxiety disorders, mood disorders, and personality disorders. Currently telehealth-only, serving clients statewide in Louisiana via the Optimantra EHR/telehealth platform. Future services (physical location, ~Month 14): Same-day urgent psychiatric appointments, walk-in crisis care, extended hours, prescription management between appointments, therapeutic plant nursery and sanctuary, workshops, meditation and mindfulness groups, horticultural therapy, retail plants and local artisan products.
product_service_summary.service_delivery: Telehealth-only currently — video visits conducted through Optimantra platform, accessible statewide in Louisiana. New patient intake via online scheduling form, brief phone screening, then scheduled within 1–2 weeks (routine) or 2–3 days (urgent presentation). At physical location: in-person outpatient clinic in Metairie, LA with walk-in urgent care capability, nursery/sanctuary space on-site for therapeutic and retail purposes.
product_service_summary.pricing_structure: Initial psychiatric evaluation: $250. Follow-up medication management: $85 per session. Hybrid payment model: accepts insurance (current panels being established) and self-pay. Sliding scale available for qualifying patients. Superbills provided for out-of-network reimbursement. Future: urgent care visit rates and group/workshop fees to be set at physical location launch.
product_service_summary.future_offerings: Urgent psychiatric care walk-in model (first in New Orleans metro). Extended and weekend hours. Group therapy programs. Horticultural therapy workshops. Meditation and mindfulness programming. Satellite urgent care sites in underserved areas. LGBTQ+ youth specialized programming. Northshore market expansion (St. Tammany and surrounding parishes).
product_service_summary.differentiators: Doctoral-level (DNP) provider with 7 years of inpatient and ED psychiatric experience — higher credential level than typical outpatient providers. First and only urgent care psychiatric model in the New Orleans metro area. Integrated therapeutic plant nursery/sanctuary with no regional equivalent. New patient access in 1–2 weeks versus the regional norm of 2–6 months. Telehealth availability for statewide reach. Trauma-informed, culturally responsive, New Orleans-rooted philosophy — provider grew up in the community and brings Hurricane Katrina survivor perspective. Accepts insurance plus sliding scale. Female-led, LGBTQ+ affirming practice.

sales_strategy.sales_process: Lead sources: referrals from therapists, counselors, pediatricians, school counselors, and community mental health organizations; Psychology Today profile; Google search. Intake process: prospect contacts practice via website or Psychology Today, completes brief online intake form, receives phone screening call within 24–48 hours, scheduled for first appointment within 1–2 weeks (routine) or 2–3 days (urgent need). No waiting list — designed for accessible, timely access. Future physical location will add walk-in urgent care channel with same-day scheduling.
sales_strategy.payment_terms: Hybrid model — accepts insurance panels (in process of credentialing) and self-pay. Self-pay rates: $250 initial, $85 follow-up. Sliding scale available. Superbills provided for out-of-network reimbursement. Payment collected at time of service. No-show and cancellation policy in place. Future: insurance billing will expand as panels are added; nursery retail will be separate cash/card transactions.
sales_strategy.retention_strategy: Ongoing therapeutic relationship through consistent medication management and supportive therapy. Crisis support between scheduled appointments reinforces retention. Care continuity is a core differentiator — not a transactional prescribing model. Future physical location with plant nursery creates community hub and wellness destination that keeps clients engaged beyond clinical visits.
sales_strategy.referral_sources: Primary channels: licensed therapists and counselors (collaborative care model), pediatricians and family medicine providers, school counselors and school-based mental health professionals, community mental health organizations, Psychology Today provider listing, word-of-mouth referrals from patients. Secondary: Google My Business listing, professional networking events, lunch-and-learn presentations to referral partners.

market_analysis.geographic_market: Primary market: Metairie, Louisiana (population ~92,000) and Jefferson Parish (population ~440,000). Secondary market: greater New Orleans metropolitan area (population ~1.27 million). Future expansion: Northshore Louisiana (St. Tammany Parish and surrounding parishes). Telehealth currently serves statewide Louisiana.
market_analysis.target_customer: Primary: Adolescents and young adults ages 12–25 with ADHD, anxiety, depression, mood disorders, and personality disorders. Secondary: Parents of school-age children ages 6–17 seeking psychiatric evaluation and medication management for ADHD, anxiety, and behavioral concerns. Tertiary: Young adults ages 18–30 with complex mental health needs including personality disorders, treatment-resistant depression, bipolar disorder, and co-occurring substance use. Future expansion: broader adult population, elderly, LGBTQ+ youth with specialized programming.
market_analysis.market_size: Louisiana ranks 48th nationally in mental health care access. The New Orleans metro area has severe psychiatric provider shortages, particularly in child and adolescent psychiatry. Typical new patient wait times in the region are 2–6 months. The national mental health care market is growing at 3–5% CAGR through 2030. Elevated rates of trauma, anxiety, depression, and substance use disorders in the New Orleans area — driven by Hurricane Katrina legacy, ongoing community violence, poverty, and systemic inequality — create sustained and growing demand.
market_analysis.industry_state: Mental health care demand nationally and in Louisiana continues to grow, accelerated by increased awareness post-COVID and persistent provider shortages. Telehealth expanded access but did not resolve the underlying shortage of qualified providers. Reimbursement trends are gradually improving for mental health parity. Child and adolescent psychiatry remains among the most critically understaffed specialties in Louisiana. Urgent psychiatric care as an alternative to emergency departments is an emerging model nationally with no existing implementation in the New Orleans metro.
market_analysis.customer_pain_points: Patients in the New Orleans metro face 2–6 month wait times for new psychiatric appointments. Many providers do not accept insurance, creating financial barriers. Existing urgent psychiatric care options are limited to hospital emergency departments — expensive, traumatizing, and designed only for acute life-threatening crises. Pediatric and adolescent psychiatric care is severely undersupplied. Patients seeking culturally responsive, trauma-informed care from providers who understand the local community context have few options.

advertising_strategy.initial_marketing: Psychology Today provider profile (already active). Professional website with SEO-optimized content. Google My Business listing. Instagram and Facebook presence. Professional networking with referral sources (therapists, pediatricians, school counselors). Lunch-and-learn presentations to referral partners. Community events and health fairs. Budget: under $2,000 for telehealth launch phase.
advertising_strategy.marketing_budget: Telehealth phase (Year 1, months 1–11): $800–$1,000/month months 1–3; $400–$600/month months 4–11. Physical location launch campaign (months 12–14): $3,000–$5,000/month. Ongoing post-launch: $1,000–$1,500/month. Year 1 marketing total: approximately $11,150. Year 2–3: approximately $15,000–$18,000 annually.
advertising_strategy.expansion_marketing: Year 2 and beyond: Google Ads campaigns targeting high-intent local search, social media advertising, video content, email newsletter for existing patients, local media and PR outreach, community partnerships and event sponsorships, nursery cross-marketing as a wellness destination, satellite site marketing as expansion proceeds.
advertising_strategy.digital_presence: Active Psychology Today profile generating new patient inquiries. Professional website (in development) with telehealth scheduling integration, provider bio, service descriptions, and SEO content targeting New Orleans area mental health searches. Google My Business listing. Active Instagram and Facebook profiles for community engagement and awareness. Future: paid Google Ads, local SEO for physical location.

competition.main_competitors: 1. Traditional outpatient psychiatric practices in the New Orleans metro — private practice psychiatrists and PMHNPs, many with 2–6 month wait times and no urgent access. 2. Community mental health centers — Jefferson Parish Human Services Authority, Metropolitan Human Services District — serving lower-income populations with high caseloads and institutional feel. 3. Hospital-based emergency psychiatric services — Children's Hospital, UMC, Ochsner EDs — designed for acute life-threatening crises only, with 6–12+ hour waits and no continuity of care.
competition.competitive_edge: Second Line Psychiatry offers what no other provider in the metro provides: a doctoral-level PMHNP with 7 years of inpatient and ED psychiatric experience, 1–2 week new patient access versus the 2–6 month regional norm, the first urgent care psychiatric model in New Orleans, an integrated therapeutic plant nursery and sanctuary, telehealth availability for statewide reach, insurance acceptance plus sliding scale, and a trauma-informed, culturally responsive approach from a provider who grew up in the community.
competition.market_gaps: No urgent care psychiatric model exists in the New Orleans metro — patients with sub-acute psychiatric crises have only hospital emergency departments, creating a costly and inappropriate care pathway. Child and adolescent psychiatric providers are severely undersupplied. No existing provider combines outpatient psychiatric care with nature-based therapeutic offerings (plant nursery/horticultural therapy). Culturally responsive care from providers with deep community roots is underrepresented.

strategy_and_implementation.business_strategy: Phase 1 (current–Month 12): Build a sustainable telehealth patient panel of 40–60 active clients. Establish and maintain 20–30 active referral relationships. Optimize digital presence and intake workflows. Refine clinical protocols. Phase 2 (Months 12–24): Identify and evaluate property for physical clinic and nursery. Develop architectural plans, zoning permits, and regulatory approvals. Complete financial planning for physical location. Phase 3 (Months 24–30): Acquire property using grant funding. Complete renovations. Hire administrative staff and nursery manager. Launch urgent care and nursery models. Phase 4 (Months 30+): Scale urgent care volume. Expand therapeutic programming. Develop nursery as a community wellness destination. Plan satellite sites.
strategy_and_implementation.near_term_priorities: 1. Reach 40–60 active telehealth clients by Month 6. 2. Build and maintain 20–30 active referral relationships with therapists, pediatricians, and school counselors by end of Year 1. 3. Submit grant application by October 2025 and secure $500,000 in funding for physical location. 4. Identify and begin evaluation of property in Metairie for outpatient clinic and nursery. 5. Optimize EHR workflows, billing, and scheduling for clinical efficiency.
strategy_and_implementation.key_risks: 1. Patient acquisition pace — if telehealth panel grows slower than projected, revenue shortfall in early months. Mitigation: active referral network development and digital marketing from day one. 2. Grant funding timeline — physical location plans depend on $500,000 grant. Mitigation: parallel pursuit of SBA and commercial lending options. 3. Credentialing delays — insurance panel credentialing can take 3–6 months and delay revenue. Mitigation: self-pay model sustains early operations while credentialing processes. 4. Provider burnout risk as solo practitioner managing all clinical and administrative functions. Mitigation: admin hire at Month 12.
strategy_and_implementation.partnerships: Referral partnerships with licensed therapists, LCSWs, and counselors (collaborative care model). Pediatric and family medicine providers for co-management of pediatric patients. School counselors and school districts for student mental health. Community mental health organizations. Future: horticulture therapy organizations, local artisans for nursery retail, community wellness organizations.

milestones.twelve_month_goals: 1. Establish telehealth patient panel of 40–60 active clients by Month 6. 2. Build 20–30 active professional referral relationships by Month 12. 3. Achieve monthly revenue of $15,000–$16,500 by Month 12. 4. Submit grant application for $500,000 by October 2025. 5. Receive grant award and begin property planning by December 2025. 6. Hire administrative assistant by Month 12. 7. Optimize digital presence and achieve consistent new patient pipeline via website and Psychology Today.
milestones.twenty_four_month_goals: 1. Acquire property and complete build-out of physical clinic and nursery by Month 24–30. 2. Launch outpatient clinic at physical location serving 60–80 active patients. 3. Launch urgent care psychiatric model — first in New Orleans metro. 4. Open plant nursery and sanctuary with therapeutic programming and retail revenue. 5. Achieve combined Year 2 revenue of $380,000 across all service lines. 6. Hire second clinical provider and full administrative team.
milestones.key_metrics: Monthly: active patient count, new patient intakes, appointment fill rate, revenue vs. projection. Weekly: new referral inquiries, no-show/cancellation rate. Quarterly: referral source tracking, net revenue by payer type, marketing ROI. Annual: Year 1 revenue target $137,500 (sales); Year 2 target $380,000; break-even on operating expenses by end of Month 6.

financial_information.cash_flow_narrative: The business launches with Dr. Richard's personal investment of $5,000 covering initial telehealth setup (EHR subscription, malpractice insurance, website, business registration). Revenue builds from $3,500 in Month 1 to $16,500 by Month 12 as the patient panel grows from ~5 clients/week to 15–20 clients/week. Operating expenses during the telehealth phase are lean ($2,000–$3,500/month), covering EHR, malpractice insurance, marketing, and professional fees. The $500,000 grant, expected in late 2025 or early 2026, funds the property acquisition, build-out, nursery setup, equipment, launch marketing, and operating reserves for the physical location. Year 2 and beyond diversify cash flow across four revenue streams: outpatient services, urgent care, workshops/groups, and nursery retail.
financial_information.financial_plan_summary: Revenue model is primarily fee-for-service psychiatric care supplemented by grant funding for capital investment and by nursery retail and programming revenue at the physical location. Telehealth phase is lean with low overhead. Physical location requires $500,000 in capital (grant-funded) and targets positive cash flow in Year 2. Year 1 sales revenue projected at $137,500. Year 2 total revenue projected at $380,000. Year 3 total revenue projected at $520,000. Break-even on telehealth operating expenses projected at end of Month 6.
financial_information.break_even_point: Telehealth phase break-even: approximately 15 active clients/week at $85/session = ~$5,100/week or $20,400/month, which covers $2,000–$3,500/month in telehealth operating expenses. Projected to reach by Month 6. Physical location break-even: approximately $30,000/month in combined revenue (outpatient + urgent care + nursery), projected by Month 18–20 of operations.

income.beginning_balance: $5,000 personal investment from Dr. Richard. Plus $500,000 anticipated grant funding (timeline: awarded Nov–Dec 2025, disbursed Dec 2025–Jan 2026).
income.client_volume: Months 1–3: approximately 5 clients/week (~20/month). Months 3–6: 5–8 clients/week (~25–32/month). Months 6–9: approximately 15 clients/week (~60/month). Months 9–12: 15–20 clients/week (~70–80/month). Year 2 (physical location): 60–80 active outpatient patients plus 3–12 urgent care visits/week.
income.annual_revenue_projection: Year 1 (sales only): $137,500. Year 1 total income including grant: $637,500. Year 2: $380,000 (outpatient $180K–$200K + urgent care $100K–$120K + workshops $35K–$45K + nursery $45K–$65K). Year 3: $520,000 (outpatient $220K–$250K + urgent care $140K–$160K + workshops $50K–$65K + nursery $90K–$110K).
income.revenue_sources: Telehealth phase: 100% psychiatric services (evaluations and medication management). Physical location: outpatient psychiatric services (~48%), urgent care visits (~27%), workshops and groups (~10%), plant nursery retail (~15%).

expenses.payroll: Owner draw: begins Month 3 at $3,000–$4,000/month (no formal payroll in months 1–2). Administrative assistant hired Month 12: $2,500–$3,000/month. Nursery manager hired Month 12–13: $2,000–$2,500/month. Year 1 payroll total: approximately $61,000. Year 2: $180,000. Year 3: $210,000.
expenses.rent_utilities: Months 1–5: $0 (home office/telehealth). Month 6–7: lease signed, security deposit paid (grant-funded). Months 8–13: $2,500–$3,500/month rent plus $500–$800/month utilities during build-out. Year 1 total: $26,200. Year 2: $42,000. Year 3: $45,000.
expenses.cogs: Telehealth phase (Year 1): $500 (minimal supply costs). Physical location Year 2: $28,000 (approximately 40–50% of nursery retail sales). Year 3: $45,000.
expenses.advertising_expense: Year 1: approximately $11,150. Months 1–3: $800–$1,000/month. Months 4–11: $400–$600/month. Launch campaign months 12–14: $3,000–$5,000/month. Ongoing: $1,000–$1,500/month. Year 2–3: $15,000–$18,000/year.
expenses.other_operating: Technology and software: $270–$490/month (Optimantra EHR, telehealth platform, scheduling, billing). Malpractice insurance: $300–$600/month. Professional expenses (licensing, certifications, CME): $220/month. Administrative supplies and miscellaneous: $200–$300/month. Year 1 total: $14,500. Year 2 (adds physical location): $30,000. Year 3: $32,000.
expenses.taxes: Estimated quarterly self-employment and income tax payments: approximately 25–30% of net income. Year 1: $8,000 estimated. Year 2 (higher revenue + property tax): $45,000. Year 3: $65,000.
expenses.loans_debt_service: $0. Business is grant-funded with no planned debt in Year 1. SBA or commercial real estate financing may supplement grant funding if needed for property acquisition; debt service would begin at physical location launch.
expenses.capital_assets: Months 1–2 (telehealth setup): $2,000–$3,000 (computer, peripherals, basic office equipment). Months 6–13 (grant-funded physical location): Clinical furniture and exam equipment, nursery fixtures and infrastructure, technology and POS systems, signage, initial plant inventory and nursery supplies. Year 1 total asset purchases: approximately $117,300 (predominantly grant-funded). Year 2: $8,000. Year 3: $10,000.
